items.text: Chapter Six
Morphological description of Tuta absoluta
Part One: General introduction to morphological description
6.1 Introduction

Morphological Description Morphological description means the study of the phenotypic and structural characteristics that characterize an organism, such as shape, size, color, body division, appendages, wings, bristles, and diagnostic signs.

Morphological description represents an important basis for identifying Tuta absoluta and distinguishing it from other pests of tomato crops, especially species that may cause similar symptoms, such as leafminer flies, potato tuber moth, tomato pinworm, and tomato fruitworm.
Tuta absoluta undergoes a complete metamorphosis, which includes four clear stages that differ in form and function:
The transition between these stages is not merely a gradual increase in size; each stage differs in body structure, behavior, location on the plant, and role within the life cycle.

The egg represents the beginning of the new individual, the larva is the stage of feeding and causing damage, the pupa is the stage of rebuilding the body and transformation, while the adult moth represents the stage of reproduction, spreading, and laying eggs.
6.2 The importance of morphological description in identifying the pest
Finding a mine inside a tomato leaf is not enough to confirm the presence of Tuta absoluta because a number of insects can form mines inside the leaves.
The presence of a larvae inside a tomato is not sufficient to rule that it is a tomato larvae, because the fruits may be infected with other types of larvae.
The correct diagnosis depends on combining a number of pieces of evidence, including:

items: The shape of the egg and where it is placed.
Having a clear head and legs.
Marks on the back of the head.
The shape of the mine and frass inside it.
The position of the pupa and the nature of its surrounding covering or cocoon.
The color of the wings and the markings on them.

items.text: The level of certainty increases whenever several compatible characteristics come together.
6.3 Complete metamorphosis in Tuta absoluta
Tuta absoluta belongs to fully metamorphosed insects, and this type of development is known as:
Complete Metamorphosis or Holometabolous Development
In it, the larvae differ clearly from the adult insect.
The larva:

items: They move inside mines and between plant parts.
Represents the phase responsible for agricultural damage.

items.text: As for the whole insect:

items: It is a small winged moth.
It does not create mines or holes inside the tissue.
It spreads the infestation between plants and fields.

items.text: The two stages are separated by the pupal stage, during which profound internal and external changes occur that lead to the formation of the adult moth.
6.4 Morphological change during the life cycle

The insect begins as a very small egg that is often deposited individually on plant parts. After the completion of embryonic development, a tiny larva emerges from it and begins searching for a suitable location to penetrate the plant tissue.
The larvae goes through four larval stages:
Successive larval instars are separated by molts, during which the larva sheds its old outer covering so the body can increase in size.
The larva does not only change in size, but changes occur in:

items: The ability of the larvae to move between plant parts.

items.text: After completing the fourth larval instar, the larva stops feeding, enters the prepupal stage, and then becomes a pupa.
After completion of metamorphosis, a small moth emerges from the pupa, completely different from the larva in body shape, wings, mouthparts, and biological function.
6.5 Morphological and biological characters
Morphological description must be separated from biological description.
Morphological description
Addresses:
Bio description
Addresses:

The two areas may be related to each other, but the length of the egg or the color of the larvae is a morphological characteristic, while the duration of egg incubation or the speed of the larvae's growth is a biological characteristic.
6.6 Variation in color and size
The color of Tuta absoluta instars is not completely constant, but rather changes depending on several factors, the most important of which are:

The new egg differs in color from the egg that is close to hatching, the newly hatched larva is different from the fully developed larva, and the new pupa differs from the pupa from which the moth is close to emerging.
This is why color is not relied upon alone in diagnosis, but rather color is linked to size, shape, where the phase is found, and other anatomical signs.
Published measurements also differ to limited degrees between studies, as a result of differences in:

items: The state of the specimen is alive or preserved.
The exact age within the instar.

items.text: When presenting measurements, an approximate range is used to express natural variation, rather than treating a single number as a fixed limit for all individuals.
6.7 Field examination and laboratory examination
Many characteristics of Tuta absoluta can be initially identified in the field using:
But some precise characteristics cannot be confirmed with the naked eye, especially:
These characteristics require a dissecting microscope, and final confirmation in difficult cases may require molecular analysis.
6.8 Collect samples without destroying them
Different phases need appropriate collection methods.
Eggs
Cut a small piece of paper around the egg instead of trying to remove it directly because slight pressure may cause it to shatter.
Larvae
The mine is opened gradually using a needle or fine forceps, avoiding pressure on the larva's body.
Pupae
It is collected along with part of the leaf, cocoon, or surrounding soil, as violent removal of the covering may damage the pupa.
Whole insects
They are collected from traps or plants, while preserving the wings and scales, because intense friction leads to the scales falling off and changing the external appearance.
Sample information must be recorded, including:

items: Type of trap when collecting moths.

items.text: 6.9 The difference between field diagnosis and taxonomic confirmation
The agricultural engineer can diagnose the infestation in the field to a good degree when the distinctive signs are met, such as:

items: Presence of frass inside the mine.
A larva with a pronounced head and legs.
The presence of a dark band behind the head in advanced larvae.
Infestation of growing tips and fruits.
Catching Tuta moths in pheromone traps.

items.text: But taxonomic confirmation becomes necessary when:

items: The pest appears in an area where it was not registered.
The results are used in scientific publishing.
An agricultural quarantine study is being conducted.
The sample is damaged or incomplete.
There are inconsistent results between the trap and the symptoms.

items.text: In these cases, the diagnosis depends on microscopic or molecular characteristics, and not on the visible damage alone.
6.10 Relationship between shape and phase location
The Find the Phase website provides a helpful guide, but it is not sufficient on its own.

Eggs may be found on leaves or young parts, while larvae are found inside leaves, stems, or fruits. The pupa may form inside a fold in the leaf, inside a silk cocoon, or near the surface of the soil. Adult moths often hide among plants, and males can be detected using pheromone traps.
However, these locations may change depending on the circumstances, so a combination of:

items: The nature of the surrounding damage.

items.text: 6.11 Diagnostic sequence of phases
Identification proceeds through the life stages from smallest to largest:
The egg
Their shape, color, size, placement, and changes are examined before hatching.
Larva
Larval age, color, head capsule, legs, prothoracic shield, body shape and distinguishing characteristics from other leafminers are examined.
Pupa
Its shape, color, length, position, presence of the silk cocoon, and changes that precede the moth's emergence are examined.
The whole insect
Body length, wingspan, body color, wings, dark spots, whiskers, antennae, and sex differences are examined.
These phases are presented in detail in the following parts of the chapter.
